knots.name: Overhand Knot
knots.useCase: The simplest stopper; the building block of many other knots.
steps: Make a loop near the end of the rope by crossing the working end over the standing part.
Pass the working end up through the loop from behind.
Pull the working end and standing part apart to tighten into a small firm knob.

knots.name: Figure-Eight
knots.useCase: A bulky stopper that keeps a rope from slipping out of a block or belay device.
steps: Make a loop by passing the working end over the standing part.
Bring the working end around behind the standing part.
Pass the working end down through the original loop to form the figure-eight shape.
Dress the knot so both strands lie flat, then pull tight.

knots.name: Square Knot
knots.useCase: Joins two ropes of equal thickness or ties off a bundle. Not for critical loads.
steps: Hold one end in each hand and cross the left end over the right.
Tuck it under and bring it back up so the ends swap sides.
Now cross the right end (now on the left) over the other and tuck it under.
Pull all four strands to tighten: right-over-left, then left-over-right.

knots.name: Bowline
knots.useCase: Forms a fixed loop that will not slip or jam; the 'king of knots' for rescue and rigging.
steps: Make a small overhand loop in the standing part, leaving a long working end.
Pass the working end up through the loop from underneath (the rabbit comes out of the hole).
Bring the end around behind the standing part (around the tree).
Pass the end back down through the small loop (the rabbit goes back down the hole).
Hold the loop size you want and pull the standing part to set the knot.

knots.name: Clove Hitch
knots.useCase: Fastens a rope to a post or rail quickly; easy to adjust but can slip under varying load.
steps: Pass the working end over and around the post once.
Cross over the standing part and wrap around the post a second time.
Tuck the working end under the second wrap so it crosses itself.
Pull both ends to snug the two turns against the post.

knots.name: Two Half Hitches
knots.useCase: A reliable, adjustable way to tie a rope to a ring, post or grommet.
steps: Pass the working end around the anchor and back toward the standing part.
Make a half hitch: wrap the end around the standing part and through the loop formed.
Make a second half hitch the same way, just below the first.
Slide the two hitches together and pull tight against the anchor.

knots.name: Sheet Bend
knots.useCase: Joins two ropes, even of different thickness or material; more secure than a square knot.
steps: Form a bight (a U-shape) in the thicker rope and hold it closed.
Pass the thinner rope up through the bight from underneath.
Wrap the thinner rope around behind both legs of the bight.
Tuck the thinner end under itself where it first emerged, on the same side as the short end.
Pull the standing parts to tighten.

knots.name: Taut-Line Hitch
knots.useCase: An adjustable loop that grips under tension; ideal for tent guy-lines.
steps: Pass the working end around the anchor and back alongside the standing part.
Make two wraps around the standing part working back toward the anchor, inside the loop.
Make one more wrap above those two, outside the loop.
Snug the coils; the hitch slides to adjust tension but grips when loaded.

knots.name: Figure-Eight Loop
knots.useCase: A strong fixed loop tied on a bight; the standard tie-in loop in climbing.
steps: Form a bight and tie a loose figure-eight in the doubled rope.
Pass the bight back through, re-tracing the figure-eight if tying around an anchor.
Dress the knot so all four strands run parallel without crossing.
Pull all strands firmly to set the loop.

knots.name: Alpine Butterfly
knots.useCase: A secure loop in the middle of a rope that can be loaded from any direction.
steps: Wrap the rope twice around your open hand to make two turns.
Move the outer turn to the inside, between the other two.
Take the new outer turn and pass it over the others and under all the wraps.
Slide the resulting loop off your hand and pull both standing parts to set it.

knots.name: Prusik
knots.useCase: A friction hitch that grips a rope when loaded and slides when relaxed; used for ascending.
steps: Form a closed loop of thinner cord with a double fisherman's knot.
Lay the loop behind the main rope and pass one end through the other to girth-hitch it.
Wrap the loop through itself two or three more times, keeping the wraps neat.
Dress the coils evenly; pull to test that it grips under load and slides when loose.

knots.name: Double Fisherman's
knots.useCase: Permanently joins two ropes or forms a cord loop; very strong and compact.
steps: Lay the two rope ends side by side, pointing opposite directions.
Wrap one end twice around the other rope and pass it back through both wraps.
Repeat with the second end on the other rope, wrapping toward the first knot.
Pull the standing parts so the two knots slide together and lock.

knots.name: Rolling Hitch
knots.useCase: Attaches a rope to a pole or another rope so it resists sliding lengthwise.
steps: Wrap the working end around the rail twice on the side the load will pull from.
Cross over the standing part and make a third wrap on the far side.
Tuck the end under that final wrap to lock it.
Pull tight; the doubled turns keep it from rolling under a lengthwise pull.

knots.name: Trucker's Hitch
knots.useCase: Creates a pulley-like mechanical advantage to cinch a load down tight.
steps: Tie a fixed loop (such as a slip loop) partway along the standing part.
Pass the working end around the anchor point and back up through the loop.
Pull down on the working end to take up slack, gaining roughly a 2-to-1 purchase.
Hold the tension and finish with two half hitches around the standing part.

knots.name: Constrictor Knot
knots.useCase: Binds tightly around an object and grips hard; great as a temporary clamp or whipping.
steps: Pass the working end over and around the object.
Cross over the standing part and wrap around a second time.
Tuck the working end under the crossing point, beneath both turns.
Pull both ends hard; the knot bites down and resists loosening.

knots.name: Timber Hitch
knots.useCase: Secures a rope to a log or cylindrical object for dragging or hoisting.
steps: Pass the working end around the log and back around the standing part.
Twist the working end around its own part three or more times.
Make sure the twists run along the rope, not just at one point.
Pull the standing part so the turns bind against the log.

knots.name: Cleat Hitch
knots.useCase: The standard way to secure a boat's line to a horn cleat on a dock.
steps: Take the line to the far horn of the cleat first, then around the base.
Cross over the top of the cleat in a figure-eight to the other horn.
Make one or two more figure-eight turns across the cleat.
Finish with a locking hitch by flipping a loop over the last horn, then pull snug.

knots.name: Anchor Bend
knots.useCase: A secure hitch for attaching a line to an anchor ring; resists working loose.
steps: Pass the working end through the ring twice, leaving a loose round turn.
Bring the end across the standing part and tuck it under both turns of the round turn.
Pull snug so the round turn clamps the tucked end.
Finish with a half hitch around the standing part for security.

knots.name: Slip Knot
knots.useCase: A quick-release loop that unties with one pull on the working end.
steps: Make a loop in the standing part of the rope.
Push a bight of the working end up through the loop rather than the end itself.
Pull the standing part to snug the loop around the bight.
A pull on the working end collapses the bight and releases the knot.

knots.name: Carrick Bend
knots.useCase: Joins two heavy ropes; lies flat, stays secure, and stays easy to untie after loading.
steps: Form a loop in the first rope with the working end on top.
Lay the second rope's end over that loop, then weave it under-over-under across the loop.
The two ends should finish on diagonally opposite sides.
Pull the standing parts; the knot capsizes into a compact, secure form.

knots.name: Water Knot
knots.useCase: Joins two lengths of flat webbing, as in climbing slings; retraces an overhand knot.
steps: Tie a loose overhand knot near the end of one piece of webbing.
Feed the end of the second piece into the knot, following its path exactly in reverse.
Keep the webbing flat and untwisted as you retrace.
Leave long tails and pull all four strands tight to dress the knot.
